description:
You are a database-reliability engineer who has to make sure that last-night’s SQL dump is safely archived and properly logged.

Current situation:
1. A single dump file already exists at  
   /home/user/data/dump.sql  
   (the directory /home/user/data/ is already present and writable).
2. No other backup-related files or directories exist yet.

Your objectives (perform them in this order; only 3–4 shell commands are needed):
1. Create a directory called /home/user/db_backups/ if it does not already exist.
2. Compress the file /home/user/data/dump.sql into a gzip-compressed archive named  
   /home/user/db_backups/dump_backup.tar.gz  
   • Any compression method is fine as long as the resulting file is gzip-compatible and the exact **archive name** is dump_backup.tar.gz.  
   • The original dump.sql must be removed once it is safely archived.
3. Produce a plain-text log file at  
   /home/user/db_backups/backup.log  
   recording what you did.  
   The log must contain **exactly four lines**, in exactly the order shown below, with a single space after each colon and no extra spaces anywhere:

   TIMESTAMP: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS  
   ARCHIVE_NAME: dump_backup.tar.gz  
   ARCHIVE_PATH: /home/user/db_backups/dump_backup.tar.gz  
   ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES: <integer>  

   • TIMESTAMP must be the current system time at the moment you create the log entry, formatted precisely as YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (24-hour clock, leading zeros where needed).  
   • ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES must be the size (in bytes, base-10 integer) of the newly created dump_backup.tar.gz file **at the time the log is written**.  
   • Make sure there is a trailing line-feed (newline) at the end of the fourth line.

What the automated checker will validate:
• The directory /home/user/db_backups/ exists.  
• /home/user/db_backups/dump_backup.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip file.  
• The original file /home/user/data/dump.sql no longer exists.  
• /home/user/db_backups/backup.log exists and has exactly four lines whose contents follow the format above.  
• The ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES value in backup.log matches the actual byte size of dump_backup.tar.gz.

No root or sudo operations are necessary. Keep the solution to 3-4 reasonable Linux commands.
truth:
Pre-existing state before the agent starts:

1. Directory: /home/user/data/
2. File: /home/user/data/dump.sql  
   Content (27 bytes including the final newline):
   This is a sample PostgreSQL dump\n
   The file’s exact byte sequence is (hex):
   54 68 69 73 20 69 73 20 61 20 73 61 6d 70 6c 65 20 50 6f 73 74 67 72 65 53 51 4c 20 64 75 6d 70 0a

Nothing related to /home/user/db_backups/ exists yet.

Expected final state after successful task completion:

1. Directory /home/user/data/ still exists but **dump.sql is gone**.
2. Directory /home/user/db_backups/ exists with two new files:
   a. /home/user/db_backups/dump_backup.tar.gz — a valid gzip archive that contains a single member named dump.sql holding exactly the 27-byte content listed above. Its size will typically be 107 bytes, but the test harness will calculate the size dynamically and compare it to the log.
   b. /home/user/db_backups/backup.log — a UTF-8 text file with exactly four lines (ending in a single newline) following the template:

      TIMESTAMP: <ISO-8601 seconds resolution>
      ARCHIVE_NAME: dump_backup.tar.gz
      ARCHIVE_PATH: /home/user/db_backups/dump_backup.tar.gz
      ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES: <calculated integer>

   The harness will:
   • Parse TIMESTAMP to ensure correct formatting.
   • Verify ARCHIVE_NAME and ARCHIVE_PATH match the literal strings above.
   • Compare ARCHIVE_SIZE_BYTES to the real byte size of dump_backup.tar.gz.

The agent must create every file inside /home/user/db_backups/ with default user permissions (umask-controlled, no root needed).
name: task_000000_b2060fbd
